content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 14, 2019

Nick Warshaw
Remcho, Johansen & Purcell, LLP
1901 Harrison Street, Suite 1550
Oakland, CA 94612

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-19-144

Dear Mr. Warshaw:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 Cal.3d 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You represent a California general purpose recipient committee (the "Organization") that makes contributions to, and independent expenditures in support of, state candidates. The Organization now also wishes to act as an intermediary for contributions to state candidates. Specifically, the Organization plans to direct its supporters to a website where they will be encouraged to make contributions (electronically and via check) earmarked for specific candidates whom the Organization has endorsed. The Organization will deduct a credit card processing fee from each contribution, along with an "administrative fee" of 2-4% of the total amount of each contribution; both fees will be disclosed to contributors prior to processing their payment. The Organization will then deposit the earmarked checks and electronic contributions into its own bank account and, within approximately one week, it will remit the funds, less fees, to the candidates for whom they were earmarked. The Organization does not intend to coordinate its expenditures with the benefiting candidates; communications with benefiting candidates will be limited to the delivery of contributions and will not include any discussion of campaign strategies or other campaign activities.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-19-144
Page No. 2

You now seek advice on how the Organization, the benefitting candidates, and donors must
report these contributions, including the fees withheld by the Organization.

QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS

Question 1: How must the organization report "administrative fees" it withholds from each
contribution?

Conclusion: Because the "administrative fees" deducted from each contribution are attributable
to the recipient candidates as fundraising expenses, the Organization will have received full and
adequate consideration, and may report the fees as "miscellaneous increases to cash" on Schedule I.

Section 85704's prohibition against earmarking provides that "[a] person may not make any
contribution to a committee on the condition or with the agreement that it will be attributed to any
particular candidate unless the contribution is fully disclosed pursuant to Section 84302." Section
84302, meanwhile, permits a person to act as an intermediary by collecting a contribution for the
benefit of another person, so long as the resulting contribution is fully disclosed as having been
made from the original contributor through the intermediary. (Section 84302 and Regulation
18432.5.)

In this case, the Organization is a general purpose committee that wishes to act as an
intermediary for contributions to state candidates. This scenario is similar to the facts in the
Mainardi Advice Letter (A-10-191), concerning ActBlue, a California general purpose committee
that processes credit card contributions from third parties for candidates through its website. Like
the Organization, ActBlue also maintains a list of candidates in a candidate directory for which it
will receive contributions. In the Mainardi letter, we concluded that "ActBlue is a 'committee'
under Section 82013 and must, therefore, be reported by a committee as an intermediary when the
committee receives contributions from ActBlue that are earmarked by a third party contributor."
Thus, like ActBlue, the Organization will be a committee intermediary and the earmarked
contributions made through the Organization must be disclosed as having been made from the
original contributor through the Organization acting as intermediary.

We have previously advised that if an organization follows proper procedures regarding the
recordkeeping and reporting of contributions earmarked for specific candidates, earmarked
contributions will count towards the original contributor's contribution limit, not toward the
organization's contribution limit. (Lucas Advice Letter, A-15-210.) Please note, however, that if the
Organization determines the amount or recipients of the contributions, then the Organization will be
considered the source of those contributions, rather than an intermediary. (Id.)

The next issue is how the Organization should report the "administrative fees" it deducts
from contributions should be reported. Ordinarily, any payment made to a committee is presumed
to be a contribution unless the payor receives full and adequate consideration, or it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that the payment is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015(a).)
Likewise, Regulation 18215(a) provides that "[a] contribution is any payment made for political
purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor."

File No. I-19-144
Page No. 3

Based on the facts you have provided, the payments to the Organization (in the form of fees)
would likely be considered to have been made for political purposes, since they will be received by
a general purpose committee, which is an "organization formed or existing primarily for political
purposes." (Reg. 18215(a)(2)(D).) Thus, the payments (fees) would be classified as "contributions"
under § 82015(a)—except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received." (Miller
Advisory Letter, A-01-263.) For purposes of the Act, "full and adequate consideration" means "fair
market value" (Section 82025.5.) We have advised that, where an exchange occurs between a
committee and a payor, and the payor receives something in exchange for paying fair market value,
the payment is not considered a contribution. For example, in the Miller Letter, we advised that
committee could sell a painting to a contributor for fair market value, and the payment to the
committee in exchange for the art would not constitute a contribution under Section 82015, but
instead should be reported as a "miscellaneous increase to cash" on Schedule 1 of the committee's
Form 460.

Miscellaneous increases to cash are transactions that increase the cash position of an
officeholder, candidate, or committee, but are not a contribution, loan, or loan repayment. These
increases must be reported on Schedule 1 of a committee's Form 460. Examples of miscellaneous
increases to cash include, but are not limited to:

• Interest received or credited to checking or savings accounts or other time deposits.
• Proceeds from the sale of property, such as paintings, furniture, or other items sold at
garage sales, auctions, etc., when the payment received is the fair market value of the
item.
• Proceeds from the sale of campaign property.
• Refunds received on deposits, such as telephone deposits; and
• Refunds from overpayment of bills.

Here, the Organization plans to charge a credit card processing fee (for electronic
contributions), as well as an "administrative fee" of 2-4% of all contributions. These fees will be
withheld from contributors' contributions, and the remaining funds transferred to the recipient
candidates. Thus, in essence, the Organization will charge the fees to the candidates. As discussed
below, this is a permissible arrangement under the Act, and we have advised that fees withheld by
processors of contributions are considered reportable fundraising expenditures attributable to the
recipient candidates. For example, in the Kanin Advice Letter (A-16-191)³, we advised that
processing fees charged by ActBlue and Democracy Engine, a similar donation processing
platform, were properly categorized as fundraising costs. By extension, here, the Organization
will receive payments from candidates for fundraising costs. Assuming the Organization's fees
constitute fair market value for the fundraising services it intends to provide, these fees would be
considered full and adequate consideration, and should be reported as miscellaneous increases to
cash on the Organization's Form 460.

³ More precisely, the Kanin Letter concluded that an Assemblywoman's legal defense fund may not pay the
fees associated with an ActBlue or Democracy Engine account, since legal defense funds are not permitted to pay
fundraising costs. However, we advised that the candidate's campaign committee could pay those costs instead.

File No. 1-19-144
Page No. 4

Question 2: Must a recipient candidate report the fees deducted by the intermediary as part
of the full amount of the contribution?

Conclusion: Yes. The entire charge authorized by the contributor, including fees deducted by
any intermediaries, must be reported as a contribution to the candidate. The fees deducted by the
intermediary must also be reported as a fundraising expenditures by the candidate.

The Act contains disclosure and recordkeeping requirements with respect to contributions
and prescribes the manner in which contributions of $100 or more may be made. (Section 84200 et
seq. and Section 84300(c).) Regulation 18421.3, meanwhile, details the reporting requirements for
contributions and expenditures collected by "contract vendors" or "collection agents." Regulation
18421.3(a) provides, in relevant part, that the "entire amount authorized by the contributor is the
amount of the contribution" received by the candidate.

The Act also permits candidates and committees to raise contributions over the Internet, as
long as the Act's disclosure and recordkeeping requirements are met. In the Iyengar Advice Letter
we advised that for credit card transactions collected over the Internet, the entire amount charged to
each contributor's credit card is the amount of the contribution. (Iyengar Advice Letter, No. A-16-
171). We further advised that "[no] committee should not substract any fees retained or charged by
the credit card company." (Id.) Therefore, for purposes of reporting contributions received on
Schedule C of a candidate's Form 460, the recipient candidates should report the total amount paid
to the Organization, including any fees the Organization withheld.

Regulation 18421.3(a) also provides that "any amounts deducted or charged by the vendor
or collecting agent are deemed to be expenditures from the [recipient's] campaign bank account at
the time the fees are deducted or charged." Thus, the processing and "administrative fees" retained
by the Organization will be considered an expenditure of the recipient candidate. Therefore, any
amounts charged or withheld by the Organization must be reported by the recipient candidates as
expenditures at the time the fees are deducted or charged.

Section 84302 and Regulation 18432.5 require intermediaries to provide a recipient with
information about a contribution so that the recipient can accurately report the contribution. The
Organization should provide the recipient candidates with the information they need to accurately
report earmarked contributions, as well as fees deducted by the Organization, which the recipient
candidates must report as fundraising expenditures.

Question 3: If a contributor is a filer themselves, how should the donor report their contribution?

Conclusion: A contributor who qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a
specifically identified committee must disclose on its campaign statements the specifically
identified committee as the recipient and the other committee as an intermediary. A contributor who
qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a specifically identified candidate
must disclose on its campaign statements the committee intermediary that received the contribution,
with a notation that the contribution was earmarked for the specific candidate.

File No. 1-19-144
Page No. 5

The Act requires campaign committees to file campaign statements and reports at regular
intervals fully and truthfully disclosing information about the committees' receipts and
expenditures. (Section 81002). Section 84211(b) requires each campaign statement to contain the
total amount of expenditures made during the period covered by the campaign statement, as well as
the total cumulative amount of expenditures made. The Act defines "expenditure" generally as a
payment made for political purposes, including payments by a committee to another committee, or
to a candidate or ballot measure. (Section 82025).

The disclosure requirements for earmarked contributions are found in Section 85704(e) of
the Act. A contributor who qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a
specifically identified committee must disclose the contribution in accordance with Section
85704(c)(1). A contributor who qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a
specifically identified candidate must disclose the contribution in accordance with Section
85704(e)(3).

Where these payments must be reported depends on the type of filer. For example, a general
purpose committee would report contributions made to other committees or candidates on Schedule
E (Payments Made) of Form 460 (Recipient Committee Campaign Statement), whereas a major
donor would report contributions made to committees and candidates on Section 5 (Contributions
and Expenditures Made) of Form 461 (Major Donor and Independent Expenditure Committee
Campaign Statement).

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Toren Lewis
Counsel, Legal Division

TAL:sal

You now seek advice on how the Organization, the benefitting candidates, and donors must 
report these contributions, including the fees withheld by the Organization.

information about a contribution so that the recipient can accurately report the contribution. The 
Organization should provide the recipient candidates with the information they need to accurately 
report earmarked contributions, as well as fees deducted by the Organization, which the recipient 
candidates must report as fundraising expenditures. 
Question 3: If a contributor is a filer themselves, how should the donor report their contribution? 
Conclusion: A contributor who qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a 
specifically identified committee must disclose on its campaign statements the specifically 
identified committee as the recipient and the other committee as an intermediary. A contributor who 
qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a specifically identified candidate 
must disclose on its campaign statements the committee intermediary that received the contribution, 
with a notation that the contribution was earmarked for the specific candidate.  
The Act requires campaign committees to file campaign statements and reports at regular 
intervals fully and truthfully disclosing information about the committees' receipts and 
expenditures. (Section 81002). Section 84211 (b) requires each campaign statement to contain the 
total amount of expenditures made during the period covered by the campaign statement, as well as 
the total cumulative amount of expenditures made. The Act defines "expenditure" generally as a 
payment made for political purposes, including payments by a committee to another committee, or 
to a candidate or ballot measure. (Section 82025). 
The disclosure requirements for earmarked contributions are found in Section 85704(e) of 
the Act. A contributor who qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a 
specifically identified committee must disclose the contribution in accordance with Section 
85704(e)(l). A contributor who qualifies as a committee and who earmarks a contribution to a 
specifically identified candidate must disclose the contribution in accordance with Section 
85704(e)(3). 
Where these payments must be reported depends on the type of filer. For example, a general 
purpose committee would report contributions made to other committees or candidates on Schedule 
E (Payments Made) of Form 460 (Recipient Committee Campaign Statement), whereas a major 
donor would report contributions made to committees and candidates on Section 5 (Contributions 
and Expenditures Made) of Form 461 (Major Donor and Independent Expenditure Committee 
Campaign Statement).
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 14, 2019 Nick Warshaw Remcho, Johansen & Purcell, LLP 1901 Harrison Street, Suite 1550 Oakland, CA 94612 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-19-144 Dear Mr. Warshaw: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 Cal.3d 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER You represent a California general purpose recipient committee (the "Organization") that makes contributions to, and independent expenditures in support of, state candidates. The Organization now also wishes to act as an intermediary for contributions to state candidates. Specifically, the Organization plans to direct its supporters to a website where they will be encouraged to make contributions (electronically and via check) earmarked for specific candidates whom the Organization has endorsed. The Organization will deduct a credit card processing fee from each contribution, along with an "administrative fee" of 2-4% of the total amount of each contribution; both fees will be disclosed to contributors prior to processing their payment. The Organization will then deposit the earmarked checks and electronic contributions into its own bank account and, within approximately one week, it will remit the funds, less fees, to the candidates for whom they were earmarked. The Organization does not intend to coordinate its expenditures with the benefiting candidates; communications with benefiting candidates will be limited to the delivery of contributions and will not include any discussion of campaign strategies or other campaign activities. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-19-144 Page No. 2 You now seek advice on how the Organization, the benefitting candidates, and donors must report these contributions, including the fees withheld by the Organization. QUESTIONS AND CONCLUSIONS Question 1: How must the organization report "administrative fees" it withholds from each contribution? Conclusion: Because the "administrative fees" deducted from each contribution are attributable to the recipient candidates as fundraising expenses, the Organization will have received full and adequate consideration, and may report the fees
